title: Streak-free glass cleaner
subtitle: A white vinegar and distilled water spray for windows and mirrors
excerpt: A streak-free glass cleaner made from white vinegar and distilled water. The 40:60 vinegar-to-water ratio cuts through grease and fingerprints without leaving a residue. Distilled water is essential; tap water minerals cause the streaks that make glass cleaners fail.
sourceNotes: UK Health Security Agency, Guidance on household cleaning products (2023), acetic acid as a surface cleaning agent. Practitioner notes on mineral deposit mechanics in glass cleaning.

recipe.batchNotes: Scales to any bottle size. The ratio is 40% vinegar to 60% distilled water.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months. The acetic acid is self-preserving and the distilled water contains no minerals to degrade. Store in an amber glass spray bottle away from sunlight.

content.text: A streak-free glass cleaner. Streaks on glass come from two sources: residual cleaning agent that hasn't rinsed off, and dissolved minerals left behind as the water evaporates. White vinegar (5% acetic acid) leaves no residue as it dries. Distilled water contains no dissolved calcium or magnesium, so nothing is deposited on the glass surface as the liquid dries. Together they give a clean, streak-free result on windows, mirrors, and glass splashbacks.

The higher vinegar ratio here (40%) versus the all-purpose surface spray (50%) is intentional; glass needs less antimicrobial punch and more evaporation speed. The vinegar smell dissipates completely within a minute of application. Essential oils are deliberately omitted; even in tiny amounts they can leave a faint haze on glass.

items.prepNote: 5% acetic acid white vinegar. 40% of the spray. Standard household cleaning vinegar.
distilled or deionised water. 60% of the spray. Tap water leaves mineral deposits on glass; distilled water does not.

content.text: Pour the white vinegar into the spray bottle, then add the distilled water. Fit the trigger-spray top. No shaking is needed; both components are miscible. Label with the contents and date.

Spray directly onto glass or mirror and wipe immediately with a lint-free cloth, a scrunched sheet of newspaper, or a microfibre cloth. Work in horizontal strokes from top to bottom. For outdoor windows with heavy grime, wipe off the bulk of the dirt first with a damp cloth, then apply the spray and polish dry. Avoid applying in direct sunlight; the spray dries too quickly and can leave faint marks before you've had a chance to polish.

Safe on: window glass, mirrors, glass splashbacks, stainless steel, chrome taps (to remove water spots). Not safe on: natural stone (the acid will etch marble, granite, and limestone); polished aluminium; screen-printed or acid-etched decorative glass panels (the acid can dull the surface over time).

12 months in an amber spray bottle. The acetic acid is self-preserving and the spray does not deteriorate. The amber bottle is used for consistency with other cleaning sprays rather than necessity; clear glass is equally fine for this particular recipe.
